title: Pide kıymalı
subtitle: Turkish flatbread with spiced minced lamb
excerpt: Pide dough stretched into a long oval, topped with spiced lamb mince and sealed into a boat shape, then baked in a very hot oven until the base is crisp, the top is bubbling, and the edges are dark and slightly charred. Turkey's answer to pizza, and arguably better.
sourceNotes: Pide is a leavened flatbread of ancient Anatolian and Levantine origin, documented in Turkish cooking literature from the Ottoman period. Kıymalı pide (with minced meat topping) is one of the most common preparations, particularly associated with the Black Sea coast and cities such as Trabzon and Samsun, where a regional pide culture with specific toppings and shapes developed. The boat-shaped form with crimped edges is documented in Turkish cookbooks from the early twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked pide for up to 2 months. Reheat from frozen in a 200°C oven for 8 to 10 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the dough and topping and freeze the extra baked pide.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made the night before and left to rise slowly in the fridge.
recipe.temperatureNote: Fan 220°C / Gas 9 — the highest your oven will go

content.text: Pide needs heat. The professional pide oven runs at 350°C or higher; it cooks a pide in 4 minutes. A domestic oven cannot reach that temperature, but cranking it to its maximum (usually 240 to 250°C) and preheating a baking tray or stone until screaming hot will get close enough. The base needs direct contact with a hot surface to crisp rather than steam.

The dough is similar to pizza dough but slightly enriched. The topping is cooked lamb mince (seasoned with onion, tomato, cumin, and paprika), spread thinly rather than piled on. The boat shape is formed by pinching the long sides up and in, then crimping the ends. An egg yolk brushed over the dough edges before baking gives the characteristic golden gloss.

items.prepNote: drained of excess liquid and crushed

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}} of flour, {{yeast-fast-action}} of yeast, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Add 240 ml of warm water and the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Mix until a dough forms. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover and leave in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled in size.

Cook the {{onion}} onion and {{pepper-green}} green pepper in a dry pan over medium heat for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook 1 minute. Add the {{lamb-mince}} and cook, breaking it up, until cooked through. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, and chilli flakes if using. Season well. Simmer for 5 minutes until the topping is moist but not wet. Stir in the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley. Let cool slightly.

Preheat the oven to 240°C (fan 220°C) with a baking tray or stone inside. Divide the dough into 4 pieces. On a floured surface, roll each piece into a long oval, about 30 cm by 15 cm. Spread the topping in a thin layer, leaving a 2 cm border. Fold the long edges up and in to form a boat shape; pinch and crimp the ends firmly. Brush the dough edges with the egg yolk. Slide onto the hot baking tray and bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the edges are deep golden and the base is crisp.

items.symptom: Base is soft and doughy rather than crisp
items.cause: Baking tray not preheated; or topping too wet, steaming the base
items.fix: Preheat the tray in the oven for at least 15 minutes; drain excess liquid from the tomatoes before adding to the topping

items.symptom: Edges unravelled and pide lost its boat shape
items.cause: Ends not pinched firmly enough; or dough overproofed and too relaxed
items.fix: Crimp the ends firmly between your fingers; bake immediately after shaping without a second proof

items.cause: Topping cooked too long before spreading; not enough tomato
items.fix: The topping should be moist when it goes on the dough; some evaporation during baking is expected, so start with a moist topping

items.symptom: Dough is dense and does not puff
items.cause: Yeast not active; dough not proofed long enough
items.fix: Check the yeast by dissolving it in warm water first — it should foam within 10 minutes; proof until the dough has clearly doubled

content.text: Pide culture in Turkey is concentrated on the Black Sea coast, where cities like Trabzon, Samsun, and Rize each claim regional variations in shape, topping, and the type of cheese or meat used. The boat shape (called kayık pide) is associated with the Trabzon region. Kıymalı (minced meat) pide is found throughout Turkey and is one of the most common preparations in the pide establishments, called pidecis, that operate as Turkish equivalents of pizzerias. A pideci serves one thing: pide, in a rotating selection of toppings, baked to order in a purpose-built stone oven. The home version is a reasonable approximation — particularly if you preheat the baking surface until it is as hot as your oven will manage.
